FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study explicitly mentioned; Data Release 4.0; 21 U.S. research sites; baseline 2016-2019; ages 9-10 years

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using temporal network modeling (Graphical Vector Autoregressive Model) across baseline, Year 1, and Year 2 follow-ups

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published 2026 in Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures time spent on specific screen activities: online chatting, social media platforms, TV watching, video streaming, videogames (separately). These isolate modalities and are not bundled without breakdown. Paired with irritability (behavioral/emotional outcome), which is individual-trait-level, not demographic-only

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports quantitative temporal network pathways: online chatting predicted decreases in irritability over time (Graphical Vector Autoregressive Model estimate); irritability predicted increased online chatting; TV and social media associated with subsequent increases in irritability

FT_IC4.evidence: Network edge weights and temporal coefficients reported for all SM-irritability associations; cross-sectional Gaussian Graphical Model also reported

FT_EC7_reason: Bidirectional design: irritability and online chatting are both IV and DV. EC7 does not apply to bidirectional analyses
notes: Longitudinal multi-wave temporal network analysis with bidirectional pathways between screen activities and irritability

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study; baseline and 2-year follow-up; n=7043; U.S. sample

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis; linear mixed-effect models assessing association of lifestyle factors with cognitive development over time

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published 2026 in Journal of Adolescence

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen time across specific modalities: passive watching, social media, gaming, social engagement (separate items). Not bundled TV+console+phone without breakdown. Paired with cognitive development outcomes (fluid/crystallized cognitive function, attention, inhibitory control, processing speed), which are individual-trait-level cognitive/neural variables

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports regression coefficients for screen time variables predicting cognitive development. E.g., negative association between passive watching and inhibitory control development; positive association between longer sleep and cognitive increases

FT_IC4.evidence: Linear mixed-effect model estimates with t/p values; sex moderation effects reported with beta coefficients and p-values for passive watching-inhibitory control association

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time variables function as IVs predicting cognitive outcomes (DV). EC7 does not apply when SM is IV in any analysis
notes: Longitudinal cognitive development study with screen time (SM, passive watching, gaming) as predictors; sex moderates some associations

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD cohort explicitly stated; adolescent brain cognitive development data set; U.S. sample

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; exploratory factor analysis on test set, validation on independent set; regression models examining risk groups and behavioral/neural outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published 2025 in The Journal of Adolescent Health

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time is one item subsumed within a composite 6-factor exploratory factor analysis (fat/sugar intake, screen time, prenatal alcohol exposure, parental aggressiveness, hyperactivity, family violence, parental education, family income). No separate phone or SM variable measured as IV or DV. No per-modality breakdown isolating phone/SM from other screen devices or activities. Screen time is bundled into a composite risk score for maladaptive behaviors; the construct does not isolate phone/SM as required by FT-IC2. Fails composite-only criterion

notes: Risk stratification study using factor analysis; screen time not separately operationalized as phone/SM variable
